zh:
阿兹特克神话是14至16世纪墨西哥谷地纳瓦语诸族——尤其是建立特诺奇蒂特兰的墨西卡人——的信仰体系。它的宇宙观建立在一笔血债之上：世界已经毁灭过四次，现行的第五太阳诞生于特奥蒂瓦坎的火堆，靠众神献出的心脏才开始运行，也要靠人血的供养才不致停摆。太阳与战争之神维齐洛波奇特利、雨神特拉洛克、羽蛇神克察尔科亚特尔与"烟雾之镜"特斯卡特利波卡构成万神殿的骨架；大地本身是原初巨兽的躯体，寻常亡者要跋涉四年才能抵达死界米克特兰的最深处。

这套神话的存世文献带着征服的伤痕。阿兹特克人自己的屏风书绝大多数毁于16世纪，今天的知识主要来自三类材料：幸存与征服后重绘的图画手抄本（《博尔吉亚手抄本》《门多萨手抄本》《博图里尼手抄本》）；方济各会士萨阿贡组织纳瓦学者以纳瓦特尔语记录的《佛罗伦萨手抄本》，以及《诸太阳传说》《库奥蒂特兰编年史》等纳瓦文献；再加上考古实物——1790年出土的太阳石与科亚特利库埃巨像、1978年后系统发掘的大神庙。站在今天墨西哥城的街心，就能亲眼看见这些刻着神话的石头。

它的气质冷硬而华丽：黑曜石镜、绿咬鹃羽、绿松石镶嵌的火蛇，配着不加讳饰的献祭逻辑。诸神很少慈眉善目，创造总以肢解与流血为代价——羽蛇神以血和骨粉造人，大地由巨鳄的躯体撕成。这种把死亡摆在文明正中央的坦然，延续在今天亡灵节的骷髅意象里；建城神话的鹰与仙人掌，则留在墨西哥国旗的正中。
en:
Aztec mythology is the belief system of the Nahuatl-speaking peoples of the Valley of Mexico in the fourteenth to sixteenth centuries — above all the Mexica, builders of Tenochtitlan. Its cosmology rests on a debt of blood: the world has already been destroyed four times, and the present Fifth Sun, kindled on a pyre at Teotihuacan, began to move only when the gods gave up their own hearts, and stays in motion only while it is fed. Huitzilopochtli of war and the sun, Tlaloc of the rain, the Feathered Serpent Quetzalcoatl, and Tezcatlipoca the Smoking Mirror form the spine of the pantheon; the earth itself is the torn body of a primordial monster, and ordinary souls walk four years to reach the deepest level of Mictlan, the land of the dead.

The surviving record bears the scars of conquest. Nearly all the Aztecs' own screenfold books were destroyed in the sixteenth century, and knowledge now rests on three kinds of material: pictorial codices, surviving or redrawn after the conquest (the Codex Borgia, Codex Mendoza, and Codex Boturini); alphabetic Nahuatl texts, above all the Florentine Codex compiled by Nahua scholars under the friar Bernardino de Sahagún, together with the Leyenda de los Soles and the Anales de Cuauhtitlan; and archaeology itself — the Sun Stone and the Coatlicue colossus unearthed in 1790, and the Templo Mayor excavated systematically after 1978. These carved stones still stand in the middle of Mexico City, in the open air and behind museum glass.

Its temper is hard-edged and sumptuous at once: obsidian mirrors, quetzal plumes, fire serpents in turquoise mosaic, joined to an unblinking logic of sacrifice. The gods are rarely gentle, and creation is paid for in dismemberment and blood — humanity kneaded from ground bones and divine blood, the land torn from a crocodilian monster. That way of setting death at the center of civilization lives on in the skulls of the Day of the Dead, while the eagle and cactus of the foundation myth remain at the center of the Mexican flag.
